summary: DHH explores the potential of dependency injection (DI) for Rails, tracing his evolving understanding from initial skepticism to growing enthusiasm. He describes how writing functional tests for Basecamp revealed real pain points that DI could solve, particularly around mocking payment gateways and mailers. After rejecting explicit registry-based approaches, he embraces Dave Thomas's idea of 'transparent DI' that would work invisibly within Rails. He endorses Jamis Buck's detailed proposal for integrating DI into Rails, while acknowledging concerns about added conceptual complexity.
key_points: DHH's interest in dependency injection evolved from theoretical curiosity at conferences to practical need when testing Basecamp's payment and email integrations
He rejected explicit registry lookups in favor of 'transparent DI' — where injection happens invisibly and developers only engage with it when they feel the pain
The proposal focuses narrowly on wiring controllers and models together, keeping DI optional rather than mandatory
DHH acknowledges that while DI might reduce code, it increases conceptual complexity — a tradeoff he's cautiously willing to make
money_quotes: DI is great for injecting mocks in hard-to-get-to places.
It's basically business as usual with DI for free. So you wouldn't even have to choose whether to do DI or not up front. Just as soon as you're feeling the pain, you start injecting.
I still wasn't really feeling the hurt that this approach would be solving in any of my Ruby work.
I think it'll even decrease the amount of code used, but the conceptual complexity will go up.
If you're uncertain of the value of injecting mocks, you can just not use it. And the world will spin around like it did yesterday.
key_insight: The best framework patterns are transparent — they should be invisible until you need them, letting developers opt in only when they feel real pain.
